definition: A subclass inherits behavior and data it does not need or cannot meaningfully support.
ruleOfThumb: If a child keeps saying 'not supported' to parent behavior, use composition instead of inheritance.

citation.author: Martin Fowler
citation.bookTitle: Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code

questions.prompt: What makes this inheritance relationship suspicious?

options.text: The child inherits operations it cannot honor, so inheritance is modeling a relationship that does not actually fit
options.explanation: Contractor is forced to override core parent behavior with failures. That signals the shared abstraction is wrong.

options.text: The issue is missing access modifiers on methods, not the class hierarchy
options.explanation: Visibility could be tuned, but it does not explain why inherited behavior is repeatedly rejected.

options.text: Throwing errors in domain methods is always invalid in enterprise systems
options.explanation: Errors can be valid; here they are a symptom of bad inheritance fit.

options.text: Employee should be an interface so this class can implement only what it wants
options.explanation: Implementing a broad interface can still force irrelevant methods. The core issue is abstraction mismatch.

questions.prompt: A team added MobilePrinter by extending OfficePrinter. Why did maintenance get harder immediately?

options.text: The subclass drags in parent capabilities it cannot support, so callers cannot rely on base behavior consistently
options.explanation: By inheriting a desktop-office feature set, MobilePrinter inherits expectations it cannot fulfill. Composition around shared print capability would be clearer.

options.text: The real issue is that printer methods should return booleans instead of void
options.explanation: Return types may improve diagnostics, but they do not fix the inheritance mismatch.

options.text: OfficePrinter should be final so subclasses cannot introduce unsupported behavior
options.explanation: Making it final avoids subclassing but does not create the right reusable abstraction for mobile printing.

options.text: The problem is that methods should be async due to I/O
options.explanation: Asynchrony is orthogonal to this design smell.

questions.prompt: Which approach is more likely to break base-type assumptions in production?

options.text: Version A — a subclass rejects inherited behavior, so code written for StorageDevice can fail when swapped with ReadOnlyArchive
options.explanation: A caller expecting any StorageDevice to support write gets runtime failure with ReadOnlyArchive. Version B models capabilities explicitly.

options.text: Version B — multiple interfaces create unnecessary complexity and should be replaced by one broad base class
options.explanation: Capability interfaces reduce invalid assumptions and more accurately represent device differences.
